glossaryTerms.definition: clay that has been through the first firing (cone 06, approximately 1000 degrees C) and is now hard but unglazed and porous
a pyrometric measure of heat-work: cone 06 is approximately 1000 degrees C, cone 6 is approximately 1220 degrees C
the trimmed-back base ridge a pot sits on

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.
Paper resist is a decorative glazing technique: shapes cut or torn from damp newspaper or newsprint are pressed onto a
bisque

surface before glaze is applied. The paper temporarily blocks glaze from reaching the clay body beneath it. After glazing, the paper is peeled away, leaving the covered areas with little or no glaze while the surrounding surface carries the full glaze coat. The paper burns away completely in the kiln, leaving no residue. The fired result is a two-level glaze surface where the resist areas are either bare clay or carry only a thin ghost of glaze, contrasting with the fully glazed surrounding areas. The piece fires to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C).
When to use it

Paper resist works on any bisqueware with a surface that paper can adhere to: smooth or lightly textured exteriors of bowls, mugs, vases, and tiles. The technique suits geometric or organic shapes that can be cut from flat paper and pressed to a curved surface without wrinkling. Very tight curves or complex three-dimensional shapes make it harder to keep the paper flat and sealed against the bisque. The resist shapes can be as small as a postage stamp or as large as a hand. The more precisely the paper is cut and pressed, the crisper the edge of the resist motif in the finished piece.
What you need

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: enough to dip or brush the full piece

content.text: Step by step

Cut or tear your resist shapes from newspaper or thin newsprint. Newspaper is porous enough to absorb the moisture that makes it adhere to bisque, and thin enough to flex around slightly curved surfaces without lifting at the edges. Avoid thick paper, cardboard, or wax-coated paper: none of these adhere reliably to bisque. Geometric shapes (circles, squares, diamonds, leaf forms) cut cleanly with scissors. Organic or irregular forms can be torn for a softer edge.
Dampen the paper shapes in a shallow dish of clean water. They need to be wet enough to be limp and flexible, but not so wet that they tear when lifted. A dip of two to three seconds is sufficient.

Wipe the bisqueware with a damp sponge to remove dust, then allow to dry for five minutes. The surface should be slightly damp, not soaking wet: too much moisture on the bisque prevents the paper from adhering properly.

Press each damp paper shape onto the bisque surface, smoothing from the centre outward to eliminate air pockets and ensure the edges are in full contact with the clay body. Any lifted edge will allow glaze to creep underneath, blurring the resist boundary. Use your fingertip to press the paper firmly onto curves.
Apply glaze over the entire surface including the paper by dipping or brushing. Dipping gives the most even coat. Hold the piece by the
foot ring
and dip to within 5 mm of the foot ring base. Lift cleanly. The paper will resist the glaze at the covered zones; glaze will settle evenly on the surrounding bisque.

Peel the paper shapes away while the glaze is still slightly wet: within one to two minutes of dipping. Use the needle tool to lift a corner if the paper has adhered tightly. Peel slowly and steadily in one direction, not straight upward, to avoid disturbing the surrounding glaze coat. The exposed areas beneath the paper will show either bare bisque or a thin residue of glaze where liquid crept under the paper edges.

Allow the glaze to dry completely. Clean the foot ring. Fire to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). The paper burns away in the kiln during the early stages of the firing, leaving no ash or residue on the finished surface.
What it looks like when it works

A successful paper resist result shows the resist motifs as areas of contrasting surface quality against the fully glazed background. Where a standard glaze surrounds the resist, the uncovered zone fires to bare stoneware or a thin glaze ghost, creating a clear visual contrast. The edges of the motif are crisp where the paper was pressed flat, and slightly softer or irregular where the paper lifted slightly or was torn rather than cut. The design has a natural, hand-made quality distinct from printed or transfer decoration.
Common mistakes

Paper lifting at the edges during glazing: the paper was not pressed firmly enough to the bisque, or the bisque surface was too dry and the paper could not adhere. Dampen the bisque very slightly before applying the paper, and press each edge firmly.

Glaze creeping under the paper: even well-pressed paper allows some glaze to wick under very narrow edges. Thicker paper (multiple layers of newspaper) gives a marginally cleaner edge. Accept some glaze bleed at the boundary as part of the aesthetic.
Paper tearing on removal: the paper was allowed to dry completely before peeling. Peel while the glaze is still slightly damp: within two minutes of application.

Glaze coat disturbed during paper removal: lifting the paper straight upward rather than peeling at an angle tears the surrounding wet glaze. Peel slowly at a low angle, holding the surrounding glaze coat flat with a fingertip.
